retrieved_docs.title: Revised Trauma Scale -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Numerous studies demonstrated the benefits of utilizing the RTS. Researchers found that after implementation of the RTS, there was no difference in the error rate for diagnosis and investigation compared to not utilizing the RTS; however, there was a statistically significant improvement concerned with the appropriateness of the formulated treatments. [7] [8] Using the RTS as a triage tool reduced the treatment errors from 21 out of 36 patients (58%) to 14 out of 54 patients (26%). [8] Additionally, researchers found that the RTS effectively predicted mortality for patients with an RTS greater than 7. [9] Furthermore, the RTS was most useful in predicting the survival rates among patients with head injuries compared to patients with penetrating or blunt traumas. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Emergency Department Triage -- Definition/Introduction -- Field and Disaster Triage
retrieved_docs.content: Another algorithm of triage is called the SALT triage or sort, assess, life-saving interventions, and treatment/transport. This is similar to the START triage system as it asks individuals who can walk to a specific area of treatment marked off for minor injuries. After, individuals not able to ambulate are asked to wave their hands to identify themselves. This allows providers to assess who can follow commands and walk, who can follow commands but cannot ambulate, and who is not able to follow commands and wave their hands. The individuals who are not waving their hands are taken care of first as they most likely need immediate medical attention, then the individuals waving their hands, then those who were able to ambulate over to the designated treatment area. Affected individuals can be divided into one of five categories based on this initial assessment; immediate, expectant, delayed, minimal, or deceased. Categorization is based similarly to the START triage system of mental status, presence or absence of peripheral pulses, and the presence or absence of respiratory distress. One difference between the SALT and START triage is that Salt asks an internal question to differentiate between immediate or expectant. The question is, "Is the patient likely to survive the current circumstance given the resources available?" If the answer is no, then the patient is deemed expectant. The benefit of the SALT method vs. the START method is that there is a grey area that is provided for the population affected and allows providers to be more flexible with their decision making. However, this could be hard on the mental health of providers who are making decisions on whether someone receives treatment or not.

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Tactical Remote Assessment and Surrogate Care -- Issues of Concern -- Surrogate Care
retrieved_docs.content: Surrogate care will differ depending on several variables. First, the situation will dictate what level of care is feasible (eg, mass casualty, hostage-taking, etc). Next is the type of communication available; this may mean direct communications across a barrier (which may be amplified with a megaphone) or using radio, cellular phone, landline, or through the phone. The surrogates must be capable of relaying information to the TMP to help guide care. There are 2 types of information that a surrogate may provide. Objective information, such as visible wounds and vital signs, is available in physical exam findings. Subjective information is supplied from the casualty or surrogate, focusing on symptoms not otherwise obtained during a rapid physical assessment. Both will add to the TMP’s situation assessment and guide medical treatment. The surrogate’s ability to transmit vital information may vary greatly, depending on their age, medical knowledge, whether or not they are injured, and psychological state. Communication should focus on brief, calm, and simple language. Thus, the TMP must give slow, clearly understood, methodical directions. The surrogate medical provider (SMP) should be directed to perform a head-to-toe assessment to determine any immediate life threats. The TCCC-endorsed MARCH algorithm (Massive hemorrhage, Airway, Respirations, Circulation and C-spine, Head injury/Hypothermia) is a rapid assessment tool that can be easily conveyed remotely. Recommendations are also that the TMP, giving medical direction, physically perform a mock assessment and interventions on another team member to ensure the delivery of clear, concise instructions. Similarly, if the TMP is directing the SMP in self-care, they should perform assessments and interventions on themselves to help with guidance. In medicine across the barricade situations, any additional information obtained about the scene should be passed on to the tactical team leader, as it may provide useful intelligence. The TMP must avoid giving away sensitive information that may give hostile actors a tactical advantage.

retrieved_docs.title: Penetrating Head Trauma -- Treatment / Management -- ED Care
retrieved_docs.content: The priority ED care goals are aggressive resuscitation, stabilization, clinical assessments, and radiological workup. Many patients with PHT will likely require operative intervention. Clinicians must have a low threshold for obtaining surgical consultation.

retrieved_docs.title: Penetrating Head Trauma -- Treatment / Management -- Prehospital Care
retrieved_docs.content: Rapid transport to a definitive care unit is crucial in the prehospital setting. The far-forward approach incorporates rapid evacuation from the “kill box” to a safer area. Critical care air transport transfers the patient to a combat support hospital (CSH) while responders provide prompt medical evaluation and resuscitation. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Glasgow Coma Scale -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: This course examines the clinical applications of the GCS in trauma triage, neurocritical care, and pediatric brain injury, with a focus on the impact of standardized assessment on patient outcomes. Comparisons with alternative scales such as the FOUR score and the GCS-P are also discussed, along with evidence-based strategies to improve interrater reliability. This activity for healthcare professionals is designed to enhance the learner's competence in clinically applying the GCS, interpreting findings, and implementing an appropriate interprofessional approach when assessing neurologic status in patients, guiding clinicians involved in neurological assessment and brain injury management.

retrieved_docs.title: Emergency Department Triage -- Definition/Introduction -- The Canadian Triage System
retrieved_docs.content: Also known as the Canadian triage and acuity scale or CTAS, is based on the NTS of Australia. CTAS is a 5-level triage system based on the severity of the illness or time needed before medical intervention combined with a standardized presenting patient complaint list. Similar to other 5 level triage systems, starting with level one as the most severe patients needing immediate medical attention, and descending in severity to level five (non-urgent). Each level of acuity in CTAS has a certain set of symptoms, including cardiovascular, mental health, environmental, neurological, respiratory, obstetrics/gynecology, gastrointestinal, and trauma. Under each category, are a list of symptoms specific to that organ system that, if present, the patient is classified under that level. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2591
retrieved_docs.content: The condition إصابة الرأس المغلقة (closed head injury) is clinically associated with تعاطي الكحول (alcohol consumption); alcohol use increases the likelihood of sustaining a closed head injury, and patients presenting with such injuries often have a history of recent alcohol intake. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians when assessing trauma patients, guiding both acute management and preventive counseling regarding alcohol use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2463
retrieved_docs.content: Unspecified site injury is a broad injury category that encompasses more specific conditions such as intracranial injury, indicating that injuries without a clearly defined location may include trauma to the brain. This category is primarily derived from the concept of multiple injuries (إصابات متعددة), reflecting that when several injuries occur together or the exact site is not specified, they are often grouped under a general “unspecified site” label. Recognizing that unspecified site injury can represent both intracranial trauma and a collection of multiple injuries helps clinicians adopt a comprehensive assessment approach, ensuring that potential brain involvement is not overlooked and that the overall injury burden is appropriately addressed in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2226
retrieved_docs.content: Traumatisch hersenoedeem is a specific subtype of أوديما دماغية, meaning that traumatic brain edema is classified under the broader condition of cerebral edema. As a form of أوديما دماغية, Traumatisch hersenoedeem involves swelling of brain tissue that results from head injury, leading to increased intracranial pressure and potential neurological impairment. Recognizing Traumatisch hersenoedeem as a subset of أوديما دماغية helps clinicians understand its pathophysiology within the context of general cerebral edema, guiding appropriate diagnostic evaluation and targeted therapeutic interventions to reduce brain swelling and protect neurological function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2205
retrieved_docs.content: FACE INJURY TRAUMA and head injury trauma are frequently observed to co‑occur with a traffic accident (حادث سير). The presence of a حادث سير strongly suggests that patients may sustain both facial and cranial injuries, highlighting the need for clinicians to assess for these specific trauma types when evaluating individuals involved in motor vehicle collisions. Recognizing this co‑occurrence improves diagnostic vigilance and guides comprehensive management of injury patterns associated with traffic accidents.

Recognizing this association aids in comprehensive assessment, timely diagnosis, and appropriate management of trauma patients following a traffic accident.

Screening for suspected neurological conditions frequently identifies abnormal CSF findings, reinforcing the importance of CSF analysis in diagnosing headache‑related disorders. Head injury (إصابة في الرأس) is associated with negative CSF findings and may lead to olfactory nerve disorder (اضطراب العصب الشمي), illustrating how traumatic brain injury can produce CSF changes and sensory deficits, guiding evaluation and management.
